chunk_summary: This section outlines urgent red flags in outpatient triage of stunted children, emphasizing acute risk identification, stabilization, and inpatient referral to prevent complications like sepsis and hypoglycemia.
learning_objectives: Identify urgent red flags in stunted children requiring inpatient stabilization.
Understand the physiological risks of severe malnutrition, including hypoglycemia and sepsis.
Apply national and IAP protocols for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Differentiate between stable outpatient cases and those needing urgent inpatient care.
key_facts: Stunting is typically chronic but can present with acute risks requiring immediate attention.
Urgent red flags include bilateral pitting edema, severe wasting, very low MUAC, lethargy, inability to feed, hypothermia, high fever, dehydration, or shock.
Severely malnourished children are at high risk for hypoglycemia, hypothermia, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
Inappropriate rehydration in severe malnutrition can lead to complications; hydration must be reassessed carefully.
National and IAP protocols prioritize facility-based care for children with edema, poor appetite, or systemic danger signs.
reference_quote: Severely malnourished children are vulnerable to hypoglycemia, hypothermia, electrolyte derangements, and sepsis. (WHO guideline on the prevention and management of wasting and nutritional oedema)
document_key_topics: Outpatient triage and acute risk identification
Urgent red flags for inpatient referral
